name: Secret des Moix
best_season: Any, as long as the sea is calm
equipment: Only helmet and lighting, unless you want to access the aquatic zone where a wetsuit is needed.
description: A coastal cave near Cala Murada featuring a large single chamber approximately 30 meters long and 19 meters wide, with a pit-shaped terrestrial entrance. The cave contains a shallow lake with marine intrusion, whose ceiling is heavily decorated with baroque-style formations including fistular stalactites and curtains. Beyond the lake lies a small secondary chamber with additional speleothems.
full_description:
The Cova Secret des Moix is one more cave with characteristics typical of those found along the coastline in this area. The cave in question is located less than fifteen meters from the sea, with access to the interior of the cavity on the left side of the cavity. The terrestrial entrance has an opening in the form of a pit of considerable dimensions, and once inside it, a second entrance gives access to the interior; the dimensions of this second entrance are 2.5 m wide by 1.40 m high, oriented 335° NNW.

Once inside, a ramp of considerable slope covered with multiple stones and "easy" to traverse — although beware of a possible slip when the stones are wet — will deposit us at the bottom of the cavity, which is completely flat and covered with a layer of sand that enters the interior of the cave dragged by the waves through the marine intrusion of the cave, not to mention what enters through the terrestrial entrance as can be verified by the remains of posidonia that can be observed at the entrance of the cave. A wide groove in the sand crosses the cave from one end to the other through which the seawater flows to feed the lake.

Moving through the cavity therefore presents no complication, since it is a single large hall of about 30 m from the entrance to the lake, which is located directly opposite the entrance opening, and a width of approximately 19 m if we do not count the small room on the other side of the lake. The hall is not concretioned except at the shore where the marine incursion "dies" and already inside the lake where some interesting concretions can be appreciated, even on the ceiling, but not in the terrestrial hall. The ceiling height in the great hall ranges from several meters at the highest point to less than one meter near the lake.

On my first visit to the cave, I could only reach the shore of the lake, leaving it pending for a later visit, which happened almost a year later. The lake has little depth; in fact, in few places does it exceed one meter in depth, but that does not make it any less interesting since it is precisely the shores of the lake as well as the ceiling where it flows that hold almost all of the cave's concretions.

From the shore of the lake, and before we venture into its waters, visible before us on the other side of the lake is a sort of gallery more than one meter wide, which raises the question of whether the cave continues through said gallery or is simply a dead end. Well, it is neither one nor the other, or yes. The narrowing or gallery is about four meters long, leading to a mini room partly covered with water, about 4 meters wide and a little more than 6 meters long, and reasonably concretioned both on the ceiling and its surroundings, with some small stalagmites protruding from the water and a small column.

Returning to the shore of the lake, we can venture into it again on the right side, leaving a column to our right as well, and directly ahead it is also possible to see dry land at the other end of the lake, which encourages us to reach it. But unfortunately, the cave does not continue much at that point; in fact, it is practically not worth getting out of the water since from it one can contemplate what remains to be seen.

At the other end of the lake there is not much to see; in fact, the ceiling is close to the water surface and offers nothing of interest.

The entire lake ceiling is heavily concretioned in a very baroque style, so I recommend looking upward while traversing its waters, where fistular stalactites, curtains, etc. abound.

As I said earlier, this cave has a marine intrusion forming a mini beach upon entering the hall next to a small column. Once past the column (as long as the sea is calm), we can still progress a few more meters through a sort of gallery.

The Cova Secret des Moix is considered a natural site of special interest, specifically speleological. J. A. Encinas S. Inventorial Relation of Natural Underground Features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

Not far from here are the Cueva de ses Figueres and the Cueva dels Ases, which we can visit to make better use of the day, since the visit to this cave, especially if we do not explore the aquatic zone, does not require too much time.
